question: 4. Let \( g(x) \) be a function that has a continuous first derivative \( g^{\prime}(x) \) for all values of \( x \). Suppose that the following conditions hold for every \( x \) : (i) \( g(0)= \) \( 0 ; \) (ii) \( \left|g^{\prime}(x)\right| \leq|g(x)| \). Prove that \( g(x) \) vanishes identically.
solution:
First Solution. We first convert the differential inequality into an integral inequality. Suppose \( x \geq 0 \). Using (i), (ii), and the continuity of \( g^{\prime} \), we have
\[
|g(x)|=\left|\int_{0}^{x} g^{\prime}(t) d t\right| \leq \int_{0}^{x}\left|g^{\prime}(t)\right| d t \leq \int_{0}^{x}|g(t)| d t .
\]

Thus
\[
|g(x)| \leq \int_{0}^{x}|g(t)| d t, \quad \text { for } x \geq 0
\]

Let \( a \geq 0 \) be chosen arbitrarily. Since \( g \) is differentiable, it is continuous and therefore bounded on any finite interval. So there is a number \( K \) such that
\[
|g(x)| \leq K, \text { for } 0 \leq x \leq a .
\]

If \( 0 \leq t \leq x \leq a \), we have \( |g(t)| \leq K \), so (1) gives
\[
|g(x)| \leq \int_{0}^{x} K d t=K x, \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leq x \leq a .
\]

Now if \( 0 \leq t \leq x \leq a \), we have \( |g(t)| \leq K t \), and (1) gives
\[
|g(x)| \leq \int_{0}^{x} K t d t=\frac{1}{2} K x^{2}, \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leq x \leq a .
\]

Continuing in this way we find
\[
|g(x)| \leq \frac{1}{n!} K x^{n}, \text { for } 0 \leq x \leq a
\]
and all positive integers \( n \).
To prove (2) formally, we use mathematical induction. We have shown that (2) is true for \( n=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( n=p \). Then for \( 0 \leq t \leq \) \( x \leq a \), we have \( |g(t)| \leq K t^{p} / p! \). Using this in (1), we find
\[
|g(x)| \leq \int_{0}^{x} \frac{1}{p!} K t^{p} d t=\frac{1}{(p+1)!} K x^{p+1}, \text { for } 0 \leq x \leq a .
\]

Thus (2) is true for \( n=p+1 \). We conclude it is true for all \( n \).
Setting \( x=a \) in (2) and letting \( n \rightarrow \infty \), we have
\[
|g(a)| \leq \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n!} K a^{n}=0 .
\]

Therefore \( g(a)=0 \). But \( a \) was arbitrary, so \( g \) vanishes on all of \( [0, \infty) \).
Consider the function \( h \) defined by \( h(x)=g(-x) \). Since \( h \) has a continuous derivative and satisfies (i) and (ii), \( h \) vanishes on \( [0, \infty \) ), by what we have already proved. Therefore \( g \) vanishes on \( (-\infty, 0 \) ] as well.

By a slight variation of this reasoning, one can prove directly inequalities of the form
\[
|g(x)| \leq \frac{1}{n!} L|x|^{n} \text { for }-a \leq x \leq a,
\]
and obtain the proof for positive and negative arguments simultaneously.
Second Solution. Suppose that \( \boldsymbol{g}(\boldsymbol{x}) \) does not vanish identically. Then from the continuity of \( g \) it follows that there exist points \( a \) and \( b \) such that \( g(a)=0, g(b) \neq 0,|a-b|<1 \), and \( |g(b)| \) is the maximum value of \( |g(x)| \) on the closed interval with endpoints \( a \) and \( b \). (For example, if \( g \) does not vanish on \( [0, \infty) \), we can let \( a \) be the least non-negative halfinteger such that \( g \) does not vanish on \( \left[a, a+\frac{1}{2}\right] \), and let \( b \) be the point at which \( |g(x)| \) attains its maximum for \( a \leq x \leq a+\frac{1}{2} \).)
From the mean value theorem it follows that there is a number \( c \) between \( a \) and \( b \) such that
\[
\left|g^{\prime}(c)\right|=\left|\frac{g(b)-g(a)}{b-a}\right|=\left|\frac{g(b)}{b-a}\right|>|g(b)| .
\]

But our choice of \( b \) shows that \( |g(b)| \geq|g(c)| \), hence \( \left|g^{\prime}(c)\right|>|g(c)| \), contrary to (ii). This contradiction proves that \( g \) vanishes identically.
This proof does not require the continuity of \( g^{\prime} \).
Remark. This is a standard result that arises in proving the uniqueness of the solutions of differential equations.

descriptive_long.question: 4. Let \( anyfunc(varreal) \) be a function that has a continuous first derivative \( anyfunc^{\prime}(varreal) \) for all values of \( varreal \). Suppose that the following conditions hold for every \( varreal \) : (i) \( anyfunc(0)= 0 ; \) (ii) \( \left|anyfunc^{\prime}(varreal)\right| \leq|anyfunc(varreal)| \). Prove that \( anyfunc(varreal) \) vanishes identically.
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. We first convert the differential inequality into an integral inequality. Suppose \( varreal \geq 0 \). Using (i), (ii), and the continuity of \( anyfunc^{\prime} \), we have
\[
|anyfunc(varreal)|=\left|\int_{0}^{varreal} anyfunc^{\prime}(vartemp) d vartemp\right| \leq \int_{0}^{varreal}\left|anyfunc^{\prime}(vartemp)\right| d vartemp \leq \int_{0}^{varreal}|anyfunc(vartemp)| d vartemp .
\]

Thus
\[
|anyfunc(varreal)| \leq \int_{0}^{varreal}|anyfunc(vartemp)| d vartemp, \quad \text { for } varreal \geq 0
\]

Let \( pointa \geq 0 \) be chosen arbitrarily. Since \( anyfunc \) is differentiable, it is continuous and therefore bounded on any finite interval. So there is a number \( boundk \) such that
\[
|anyfunc(varreal)| \leq boundk, \text { for } 0 \leq varreal \leq pointa .
\]

If \( 0 \leq vartemp \leq varreal \leq pointa \), we have \( |anyfunc(vartemp)| \leq boundk \), so (1) gives
\[
|anyfunc(varreal)| \leq \int_{0}^{varreal} boundk d vartemp=boundk varreal, \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leq varreal \leq pointa .
\]

Now if \( 0 \leq vartemp \leq varreal \leq pointa \), we have \( |anyfunc(vartemp)| \leq boundk vartemp \), and (1) gives
\[
|anyfunc(varreal)| \leq \int_{0}^{varreal} boundk vartemp d vartemp=\frac{1}{2} boundk varreal^{2}, \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leq varreal \leq pointa .
\]

Continuing in this way we find
\[
|anyfunc(varreal)| \leq \frac{1}{indexn!} boundk varreal^{indexn}, \text { for } 0 \leq varreal \leq pointa
\]
and all positive integers \( indexn \).
To prove (2) formally, we use mathematical induction. We have shown that (2) is true for \( indexn=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( indexn=indexp \). Then for \( 0 \leq vartemp \leq  varreal \leq pointa \), we have \( |anyfunc(vartemp)| \leq boundk vartemp^{indexp} / indexp! \). Using this in (1), we find
\[
|anyfunc(varreal)| \leq \int_{0}^{varreal} \frac{1}{indexp!} boundk vartemp^{indexp} d vartemp=\frac{1}{(indexp+1)!} boundk varreal^{indexp+1}, \text { for } 0 \leq varreal \leq pointa .
\]

Thus (2) is true for \( indexn=indexp+1 \). We conclude it is true for all \( indexn \).
Setting \( varreal=pointa \) in (2) and letting \( indexn \rightarrow \infty \), we have
\[
|anyfunc(pointa)| \leq \lim _{indexn \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{indexn!} boundk pointa^{indexn}=0 .
\]

Therefore \( anyfunc(pointa)=0 \). But \( pointa \) was arbitrary, so \( anyfunc \) vanishes on all of \( [0, \infty) \).
Consider the function \( reflect \) defined by \( reflect(varreal)=anyfunc(-varreal) \). Since \( reflect \) has a continuous derivative and satisfies (i) and (ii), \( reflect \) vanishes on \( [0, \infty ) \), by what we have already proved. Therefore \( anyfunc \) vanishes on \( (-\infty, 0 ] \) as well.

By a slight variation of this reasoning, one can prove directly inequalities of the form
\[
|anyfunc(varreal)| \leq \frac{1}{indexn!} boundl|varreal|^{indexn} \text { for }-pointa \leq varreal \leq pointa,
\]
and obtain the proof for positive and negative arguments simultaneously.
Second Solution. Suppose that \( \boldsymbol{anyfunc}(\boldsymbol{varreal}) \) does not vanish identically. Then from the continuity of \( anyfunc \) it follows that there exist points \( pointa \) and \( pointb \) such that \( anyfunc(pointa)=0, anyfunc(pointb) \neq 0,|pointa-pointb|<1 \), and \( |anyfunc(pointb)| \) is the maximum value of \( |anyfunc(varreal)| \) on the closed interval with endpoints \( pointa \) and \( pointb \). (For example, if \( anyfunc \) does not vanish on \( [0, \infty) \), we can let \( pointa \) be the least non-negative halfinteger such that \( anyfunc \) does not vanish on \( \left[pointa, pointa+\frac{1}{2}\right] \), and let \( pointb \) be the point at which \( |anyfunc(varreal)| \) attains its maximum for \( pointa \leq varreal \leq pointa+\frac{1}{2} \).)
From the mean value theorem it follows that there is a number \( pointc \) between \( pointa \) and \( pointb \) such that
\[
\left|anyfunc^{\prime}(pointc)\right|=\left|\frac{anyfunc(pointb)-anyfunc(pointa)}{pointb-pointa}\right|=\left|\frac{anyfunc(pointb)}{pointb-pointa}\right|>|anyfunc(pointb)| .
\]

But our choice of \( pointb \) shows that \( |anyfunc(pointb)| \geq|anyfunc(pointc)| \), hence \( \left|anyfunc^{\prime}(pointc)\right|>|anyfunc(pointc)| \), contrary to (ii). This contradiction proves that \( anyfunc \) vanishes identically.
This proof does not require the continuity of \( anyfunc^{\prime} \).
Remark. This is a standard result that arises in proving the uniqueness of the solutions of differential equations.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 4. Let \( pinecones(tortoises) \) be a function that has a continuous first derivative \( pinecones^{\prime}(tortoises) \) for all values of \( tortoises \). Suppose that the following conditions hold for every \( tortoises \) : (i) \( pinecones(0)= 0 ;\) (ii) \( \left|pinecones^{\prime}(tortoises)\right| \leq|pinecones(tortoises)| \). Prove that \( pinecones(tortoises) \) vanishes identically.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. We first convert the differential inequality into an integral inequality. Suppose \( tortoises \geq 0 \). Using (i), (ii), and the continuity of \( pinecones^{\prime} \), we have
\[
|pinecones(tortoises)|=\left|\int_{0}^{tortoises} pinecones^{\prime}(skylights) \, d skylights\right| \leq \int_{0}^{tortoises}\left|pinecones^{\prime}(skylights)\right| \, d skylights \leq \int_{0}^{tortoises}|pinecones(skylights)| \, d skylights .
\]
Thus
\[
|pinecones(tortoises)| \leq \int_{0}^{tortoises}|pinecones(skylights)| \, d skylights, \quad \text { for } tortoises \geq 0
\]
Let \( paintbrush \geq 0 \) be chosen arbitrarily. Since \( pinecones \) is differentiable, it is continuous and therefore bounded on any finite interval. So there is a number \( blueberry \) such that
\[
|pinecones(tortoises)| \leq blueberry, \text { for } 0 \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush .
\]
If \( 0 \leq skylights \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush \), we have \( |pinecones(skylights)| \leq blueberry \), so (1) gives
\[
|pinecones(tortoises)| \leq \int_{0}^{tortoises} blueberry \, d skylights=blueberry\,tortoises, \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush .
\]
Now if \( 0 \leq skylights \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush \), we have \( |pinecones(skylights)| \leq blueberry\,skylights \), and (1) gives
\[
|pinecones(tortoises)| \leq \int_{0}^{tortoises} blueberry\,skylights \, d skylights=\frac{1}{2} blueberry\,tortoises^{2}, \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush .
\]
Continuing in this way we find
\[
|pinecones(tortoises)| \leq \frac{1}{dragonfly!} \, blueberry\,tortoises^{dragonfly}, \text { for } 0 \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush
\]
and all positive integers \( dragonfly \).
To prove (2) formally, we use mathematical induction. We have shown that (2) is true for \( dragonfly=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( dragonfly=quarterback \). Then for \( 0 \leq skylights \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush \), we have \( |pinecones(skylights)| \leq blueberry\,skylights^{quarterback} / quarterback! \). Using this in (1), we find
\[
|pinecones(tortoises)| \leq \int_{0}^{tortoises} \frac{1}{quarterback!} \, blueberry\,skylights^{quarterback} \, d skylights=\frac{1}{(quarterback+1)!} \, blueberry\,tortoises^{quarterback+1}, \text { for } 0 \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush .
\]
Thus (2) is true for \( dragonfly=quarterback+1 \). We conclude it is true for all \( dragonfly \).
Setting \( tortoises=paintbrush \) in (2) and letting \( dragonfly \rightarrow \infty \), we have
\[
|pinecones(paintbrush)| \leq \lim _{dragonfly \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{dragonfly!} \, blueberry\,paintbrush^{dragonfly}=0 .
\]
Therefore \( pinecones(paintbrush)=0 \). But \( paintbrush \) was arbitrary, so \( pinecones \) vanishes on all of \( [0, \infty) \).
Consider the function \( marshland \) defined by \( marshland(tortoises)=pinecones(-tortoises) \). Since \( marshland \) has a continuous derivative and satisfies (i) and (ii), \( marshland \) vanishes on \( [0, \infty) \), by what we have already proved. Therefore \( pinecones \) vanishes on \( (-\infty, 0 ] \) as well.

By a slight variation of this reasoning, one can prove directly inequalities of the form
\[
|pinecones(tortoises)| \leq \frac{1}{dragonfly!} \, snowflake|tortoises|^{dragonfly} \text { for }-paintbrush \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush,
\]
and obtain the proof for positive and negative arguments simultaneously.

Second Solution. Suppose that \( \boldsymbol{pinecones}(\boldsymbol{tortoises}) \) does not vanish identically. Then from the continuity of \( pinecones \) it follows that there exist points \( paintbrush \) and \( copperwire \) such that \( pinecones(paintbrush)=0,\; pinecones(copperwire) \neq 0,\; |paintbrush-copperwire|<1 \), and \( |pinecones(copperwire)| \) is the maximum value of \( |pinecones(tortoises)| \) on the closed interval with endpoints \( paintbrush \) and \( copperwire \). (For example, if \( pinecones \) does not vanish on \( [0, \infty) \), we can let \( paintbrush \) be the least non-negative half-integer such that \( pinecones \) does not vanish on \( [paintbrush, paintbrush+\tfrac{1}{2}] \), and let \( copperwire \) be the point at which \( |pinecones(tortoises)| \) attains its maximum for \( paintbrush \leq tortoises \leq paintbrush+\tfrac{1}{2} \).)
From the mean value theorem it follows that there is a number \( sandcastle \) between \( paintbrush \) and \( copperwire \) such that
\[
\left|pinecones^{\prime}(sandcastle)\right|=\left|\frac{pinecones(copperwire)-pinecones(paintbrush)}{copperwire-paintbrush}\right|=\left|\frac{pinecones(copperwire)}{copperwire-paintbrush}\right|>|pinecones(copperwire)| .
\]
But our choice of \( copperwire \) shows that \( |pinecones(copperwire)| \geq|pinecones(sandcastle)| \), hence \( \left|pinecones^{\prime}(sandcastle)\right|>|pinecones(sandcastle)| \), contrary to (ii). This contradiction proves that \( pinecones \) vanishes identically.
This proof does not require the continuity of \( pinecones^{\prime} \).
Remark. This is a standard result that arises in proving the uniqueness of the solutions of differential equations.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 4. Let \( nonvarying(constant) \) be a function that has a continuous first derivative \( nonvarying^{\prime}(constant) \) for all values of \( constant \). Suppose that the following conditions hold for every \( constant \) : (i) \( nonvarying(0)=0 ;\) (ii) \( \left|nonvarying^{\prime}(constant)\right| \leq|nonvarying(constant)| \). Prove that \( nonvarying(constant) \) vanishes identically.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. We first convert the differential inequality into an integral inequality. Suppose \( constant \geq 0 \). Using (i), (ii), and the continuity of \( nonvarying^{\prime} \), we have
\[
|nonvarying(constant)|=\left|\int_{0}^{constant} nonvarying^{\prime}(fixedtime)\, d fixedtime\right| \leq \int_{0}^{constant}\left|nonvarying^{\prime}(fixedtime)\right|\, d fixedtime \leq \int_{0}^{constant}|nonvarying(fixedtime)|\, d fixedtime .
\]

Thus
\[
|nonvarying(constant)| \leq \int_{0}^{constant}|nonvarying(fixedtime)|\, d fixedtime, \quad \text { for } constant \geq 0
\]

Let \( limitless \geq 0 \) be chosen arbitrarily. Since \( nonvarying \) is differentiable, it is continuous and therefore bounded on any finite interval. So there is a number \( unbounded \) such that
\[
|nonvarying(constant)| \leq unbounded, \text { for } 0 \leq constant \leq limitless .
\]

If \( 0 \leq fixedtime \leq constant \leq limitless \), we have \( |nonvarying(fixedtime)| \leq unbounded \), so (1) gives
\[
|nonvarying(constant)| \leq \int_{0}^{constant} unbounded\, d fixedtime = unbounded\, constant, \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leq constant \leq limitless .
\]

Now if \( 0 \leq fixedtime \leq constant \leq limitless \), we have \( |nonvarying(fixedtime)| \leq unbounded\, fixedtime \), and (1) gives
\[
|nonvarying(constant)| \leq \int_{0}^{constant} unbounded\, fixedtime\, d fixedtime =\frac{1}{2}\, unbounded\, constant^{2}, \quad \text { for } \quad 0 \leq constant \leq limitless .
\]

Continuing in this way we find
\[
|nonvarying(constant)| \leq \frac{1}{continuum!}\, unbounded\, constant^{continuum}, \text { for } 0 \leq constant \leq limitless
\]
and all positive integers \( continuum \).

To prove (2) formally, we use mathematical induction. We have shown that (2) is true for \( continuum=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( continuum=fluidity \). Then for \( 0 \leq fixedtime \leq constant \leq limitless \), we have \( |nonvarying(fixedtime)| \leq unbounded\, fixedtime^{fluidity} / fluidity! \). Using this in (1), we find
\[
|nonvarying(constant)| \leq \int_{0}^{constant} \frac{1}{fluidity!}\, unbounded\, fixedtime^{fluidity}\, d fixedtime = \frac{1}{(fluidity+1)!}\, unbounded\, constant^{fluidity+1}, \text { for } 0 \leq constant \leq limitless .
\]

Thus (2) is true for \( continuum=fluidity+1 \). We conclude it is true for all \( continuum \).

Setting \( constant=limitless \) in (2) and letting \( continuum \rightarrow \infty \), we have
\[
|nonvarying(limitless)| \leq \lim _{continuum \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{continuum!}\, unbounded\, limitless^{continuum}=0 .
\]

Therefore \( nonvarying(limitless)=0 \). But \( limitless \) was arbitrary, so \( nonvarying \) vanishes on all of \( [0, \infty) \).

Consider the function \( inertvalue \) defined by \( inertvalue(constant)=nonvarying(-constant) \). Since \( inertvalue \) has a continuous derivative and satisfies (i) and (ii), \( inertvalue \) vanishes on \( [0, \infty) \), by what we have already proved. Therefore \( nonvarying \) vanishes on \( (-\infty, 0] \) as well.

By a slight variation of this reasoning, one can prove directly inequalities of the form
\[
|nonvarying(constant)| \leq \frac{1}{continuum!}\, variable\, |constant|^{continuum} \text { for } -limitless \leq constant \leq limitless,
\]
and obtain the proof for positive and negative arguments simultaneously.

Second Solution. Suppose that \( \boldsymbol{nonvarying}(\boldsymbol{constant}) \) does not vanish identically. Then from the continuity of \( nonvarying \) it follows that there exist points \( limitless \) and \( boundless \) such that \( nonvarying(limitless)=0,\, nonvarying(boundless) \neq 0,\, |limitless-boundless|<1 \), and \( |nonvarying(boundless)| \) is the maximum value of \( |nonvarying(constant)| \) on the closed interval with endpoints \( limitless \) and \( boundless \). (For example, if \( nonvarying \) does not vanish on \( [0, \infty) \), we can let \( limitless \) be the least non-negative half-integer such that \( nonvarying \) does not vanish on \( \left[limitless,\, limitless+\frac{1}{2}\right] \), and let \( boundless \) be the point at which \( |nonvarying(constant)| \) attains its maximum for \( limitless \leq constant \leq limitless+\frac{1}{2} \).)

From the mean value theorem it follows that there is a number \( restricted \) between \( limitless \) and \( boundless \) such that
\[
\left|nonvarying^{\prime}(restricted)\right|=\left|\frac{nonvarying(boundless)-nonvarying(limitless)}{boundless-limitless}\right|=\left|\frac{nonvarying(boundless)}{boundless-limitless}\right|>|nonvarying(boundless)| .
\]

But our choice of \( boundless \) shows that \( |nonvarying(boundless)| \geq |nonvarying(restricted)| \), hence \( \left|nonvarying^{\prime}(restricted)\right| > |nonvarying(restricted)| \), contrary to (ii). This contradiction proves that \( nonvarying \) vanishes identically.

This proof does not require the continuity of \( nonvarying^{\prime} \).

Remark. This is a standard result that arises in proving the uniqueness of the solutions of differential equations.

garbled_string.question: 4. Let \( qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe) \) be a function that has a continuous first derivative \( qzxwvtnp^{\prime}(lsfdkjwe) \) for all values of \( lsfdkjwe \). Suppose that the following conditions hold for every \( lsfdkjwe \) : (i) \( qzxwvtnp(0)= \) 0 ; (ii) \( \left|qzxwvtnp^{\prime}(lsfdkjwe)\right| \leq |qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)| \). Prove that \( qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe) \) vanishes identically.
garbled_string.solution:
First Solution. We first convert the differential inequality into an integral inequality. Suppose \( lsfdkjwe \geq 0 \). Using (i), (ii), and the continuity of \( qzxwvtnp^{\prime} \), we have
\[
|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)|=\left|\int_{0}^{lsfdkjwe} qzxwvtnp^{\prime}(prxvence) \, dprxvence\right| \leq \int_{0}^{lsfdkjwe}\left|qzxwvtnp^{\prime}(prxvence)\right| \, dprxvence \leq \int_{0}^{lsfdkjwe}|qzxwvtnp(prxvence)| \, dprxvence .
\]
Thus
\[
|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)| \leq \int_{0}^{lsfdkjwe}|qzxwvtnp(prxvence)| \, dprxvence, \quad \text{for } lsfdkjwe \geq 0. \tag{1}
\]
Let \( vbgncmlq \geq 0 \) be chosen arbitrarily. Since \( qzxwvtnp \) is differentiable, it is continuous and therefore bounded on any finite interval. So there is a number \( dfghjklo \) such that
\[
|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)| \leq dfghjklo, \qquad 0 \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq .
\]
If \( 0 \leq prxvence \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq \), we have \( |qzxwvtnp(prxvence)| \leq dfghjklo \); using (1) gives
\[
|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)| \leq \int_{0}^{lsfdkjwe} dfghjklo \, dprxvence = dfghjklo\, lsfdkjwe, \qquad 0 \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq .
\]
Now if \( 0 \leq prxvence \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq \), we have \( |qzxwvtnp(prxvence)| \leq dfghjklo\, prxvence \), and (1) gives
\[
|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)| \leq \int_{0}^{lsfdkjwe} dfghjklo\, prxvence \, dprxvence = \frac{1}{2}dfghjklo\, lsfdkjwe^{2}, \qquad 0 \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq .
\]
Continuing in this way we find
\[
|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)| \leq \frac{1}{zpqoridv!}dfghjklo\, lsfdkjwe^{zpqoridv}, \qquad 0 \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq, \tag{2}
\]
for all positive integers \( zpqoridv \).

To prove (2) formally, we use mathematical induction. We have shown that (2) is true for \( zpqoridv=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( zpqoridv=ugtiwcsr \). Then for \( 0 \leq prxvence \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq \) we have \( |qzxwvtnp(prxvence)| \leq dfghjklo\, prxvence^{ugtiwcsr}/ugtiwcsr! \). Using this in (1), we find
\[
|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)| \leq \int_{0}^{lsfdkjwe} \frac{dfghjklo}{ugtiwcsr!} prxvence^{ugtiwcsr} \, dprxvence = \frac{dfghjklo}{(ugtiwcsr+1)!} lsfdkjwe^{ugtiwcsr+1}, \qquad 0 \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq .
\]
Thus (2) holds for \( zpqoridv=ugtiwcsr+1 \), and hence for all \( zpqoridv \).
Setting \( lsfdkjwe = vbgncmlq \) in (2) and letting \( zpqoridv \to \infty \), we obtain
\[
|qzxwvtnp(vbgncmlq)| \leq \lim_{zpqoridv\to\infty} \frac{dfghjklo\, vbgncmlq^{zpqoridv}}{zpqoridv!}=0.
\]
Therefore \( qzxwvtnp(vbgncmlq)=0 \). Since \( vbgncmlq \) was arbitrary, \( qzxwvtnp \) vanishes on all of \([0,\infty)\).

Consider the function \( hjgrksla \) defined by \( hjgrksla(lsfdkjwe)=qzxwvtnp(-lsfdkjwe) \). Because \( hjgrksla \) has a continuous derivative and satisfies (i) and (ii), \( hjgrksla \) vanishes on \([0,\infty)\). Hence \( qzxwvtnp \) vanishes on \(( -\infty,0] \) as well.

By a slight variation of this reasoning one can prove directly inequalities of the form
\[
|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)| \leq \frac{1}{zpqoridv!} cvmnxzas |lsfdkjwe|^{zpqoridv}, \qquad -vbgncmlq \leq lsfdkjwe \leq vbgncmlq,
\]
and obtain the proof for positive and negative arguments simultaneously.

Second Solution. Suppose that \( \boldsymbol{qzxwvtnp}(\boldsymbol{lsfdkjwe}) \) does not vanish identically. By continuity there exist points \( vbgncmlq \) and \( wrethspo \) such that \( qzxwvtnp(vbgncmlq)=0, \; qzxwvtnp(wrethspo)\neq0, \; |vbgncmlq-wrethspo|<1, \) and \( |qzxwvtnp(wrethspo)| \) is the maximum of \(|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)|\) on the closed interval with endpoints \( vbgncmlq \) and \( wrethspo \). (For instance, if \( qzxwvtnp \) does not vanish on \([0,\infty)\), choose \( vbgncmlq \) to be the least non-negative half-integer such that \( qzxwvtnp \) does not vanish on \([vbgncmlq, vbgncmlq+\tfrac12]\), and let \( wrethspo \) be the point where \(|qzxwvtnp(lsfdkjwe)|\) attains its maximum on this interval.)

By the mean-value theorem there exists \( mxnvjkal \) between \( vbgncmlq \) and \( wrethspo \) such that
\[
|qzxwvtnp^{\prime}(mxnvjkal)| = \left|\frac{qzxwvtnp(wrethspo)-qzxwvtnp(vbgncmlq)}{wrethspo-vbgncmlq}\right| = \left|\frac{qzxwvtnp(wrethspo)}{wrethspo-vbgncmlq}\right| > |qzxwvtnp(wrethspo)|.
\]
Because \( |qzxwvtnp(wrethspo)| \ge |qzxwvtnp(mxnvjkal)| \), we obtain \( |qzxwvtnp^{\prime}(mxnvjkal)| > |qzxwvtnp(mxnvjkal)| \), contradicting (ii). Hence \( qzxwvtnp \) must vanish identically.

This proof does not require the continuity of \( qzxwvtnp^{\prime} \).

Remark. This is a standard result that arises in proving the uniqueness of solutions of differential equations.

kernel_variant.question:
Let H be a (possibly infinite-dimensional) real separable Hilbert space with inner product \langle \cdot ,\cdot \rangle  and induced norm \|\cdot \|.  

Let  

  g : H \to  H  

be Frechet-differentiable at every point and assume that its derivative  

  Dg : H \to  B(H), x \mapsto  Dg(x)  

is continuous, where B(H) denotes the Banach space of bounded linear operators on H endowed with the operator norm \|\cdot \|op.  
Suppose  

(1) g(0)=0,  

(2) \|Dg(x)\|op \leq  \|g(x)\| for every x \in  H.  

Prove that g vanishes identically on H; that is, g(x)=0 for every x\in H.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the proof we repeatedly use the following basic facts.

* If \gamma :[0,1]\to H is C^1, then by the fundamental theorem of calculus for Bochner-valued functions  
  \gamma (1)-\gamma (0)=\int _0^1\gamma '(t) dt  (Bochner integral).  
* The norm \|\cdot \| on a Hilbert space is 1-Lipschitz and therefore absolutely continuous along absolutely continuous curves; in particular, if u:[0,1]\to H is absolutely continuous, then t\mapsto \|u(t)\| is also absolutely continuous and differentiable almost everywhere (a.e.).

Step 1.  Integral representation of g(x).  
Fix x\in H and consider the C^1 line \gamma (t)=tx (0\leq t\leq 1).  By the chain rule

  d/dt g(\gamma (t)) = Dg(tx)[x] for every t\in [0,1].

Using g(0)=0 and applying the Bochner fundamental theorem of calculus we obtain

  g(x)=\int _0^1Dg(tx)[x] dt.  (3)

Step 2.  An integral inequality for \|g(x)\|.  
Taking norms in (3) and using (2) gives

  \|g(x)\| \leq  \int _0^1\|Dg(tx)\|op\|x\| dt \leq  \|x\|\int _0^1\|g(tx)\| dt.  (4)

For the fixed x set  

  f(t):=\|g(tx)\| (0\leq t\leq 1).

Then (4) rewrites as

  f(1) \leq  \|x\|\int _0^1f(t) dt.  (5)

Step 3.  Absolute continuity of f and a differential inequality.  
Because t\mapsto g(tx) is C^1, the mapping f(t)=\|g(tx)\| is the composition of a C^1 map with the Lipschitz norm. Hence f is absolutely continuous on [0,1] and therefore differentiable a.e.

For a.e. t with g(tx)\neq 0 we have by the chain rule for a norm,

  f '(t)=\langle Dg(tx)[x], g(tx)/\|g(tx)\|\rangle .

If g(tx)=0 we simply keep the convention f'(t)=0; this causes no harm because such t belong to a set on which f vanishes identically and hence f is flat there.  Estimate (2) then yields, for a.e. t\in [0,1],

  |f '(t)| \leq  \|Dg(tx)\|op\|x\| \leq  \|g(tx)\|\|x\| = f(t)\|x\|.  (6)

Combining with sign information we may write

  f '(t) \leq  \|x\|f(t)  a.e. on [0,1],   f(0)=0.  (7)

Step 4.  Gronwall-type argument.  
Define h(t):=e^{-\|x\|t}f(t).  Equation (7) implies, for a.e. t,

  h '(t)=e^{-\|x\|t}(f '(t)-\|x\|f(t)) \leq  0.  (8)

Thus h is absolutely continuous and non-increasing.  Since h(0)=f(0)=0 and h\geq 0, we have h(t)=0 for every t\in [0,1].  Consequently f(t)=0 for all t, and in particular f(1)=\|g(x)\|=0.

Step 5.  Conclusion.  
Because the choice of x\in H was arbitrary, we conclude that \|g(x)\|=0 for every x\in H; hence g(x)=0 for every x\in H, as required.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher‐dimensional / infinite‐dimensional setting  
   • The problem is posed on an arbitrary separable Hilbert space, not on ℝ; the derivative is the full Fréchet derivative, a bounded linear operator, and norms are operator norms.  
2. Additional layers of structure  
   • The solution must manipulate operator norms, Fréchet differentiability, and path integrals in Banach spaces, none of which appear in the original problem.  
3. Advanced techniques required  
   • One has to convert the operator inequality into a scalar inequality along arbitrary rays in H, justify differentiability of composed maps, integrate operator‐valued functions, and employ the Grönwall lemma in an abstract setting.  
4. Non-elementary tools  
   • Knowledge of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus in Banach spaces, continuity of the Fréchet derivative, and the differential form of Grönwall’s lemma are indispensable.  
5. More steps and subtler reasoning  
   • The argument now involves: (a) rewriting g via an integral of its Fréchet derivative, (b) turning an operator inequality into a scalar differential inequality, (c) transforming that inequality with an exponential integrating factor, and (d) concluding with Grönwall.  
These additions make the enhanced variant significantly tougher than the original single-variable, first-derivative inequality.
